questions.answer: A. The function call fX("a b c d") will always return a value
questions.to_find: Which statements about the C function fX are TRUE

understand.plain: The function fX takes a char pointer, advances pointer b until it hits the null terminator '\0', then returns the difference between end and start pointers. This is essentially computing the length of the string. We need to evaluate which of the given statements about its behavior are correct.

keywords.explain: Subtracting two pointers of the same type gives the number of elements between them
keywords.example: if b points to index 4 and a points to index 0 of same array, b-a = 4

keywords.explain: In C, strings end with '\0' (ASCII 0). The while(*b) loop stops when *b is '\0'
A string literal like "a b c d" is stored in read-only memory but is a valid char pointer
keywords.example: fX("hello") passes a pointer to the literal; b-a returns 5

understand.visual_alt: fX walks pointer b from start of string to null terminator, returns distance = string length

given.aim: Determine which statements about function fX (which computes string length) are TRUE

terms.meaning: Function receiving a char pointer (start of a C string)
terms.connects: The function computes length by pointer difference

terms.meaning: Advances b until it hits the null terminator '\0'
terms.example: For "abcd", b stops at the '\0' after 'd', 4 steps later
terms.connects: After loop, b - a equals the number of characters before null

terms.meaning: Pointer difference = number of chars between a and b = string length
terms.connects: This is the return value; valid whenever a points to a null-terminated string

given.plan: Evaluate each option: A passes a string literal (valid, always returns 7 for "a b c d"), B checks if fX(c) returns exactly 4 for c[]="abcd" (yes, length is 4), C checks compilation (no issue, valid C), D checks if fX(c) always returns a value for c[]="abcd" (yes, always returns 4).

steps.title: Understand what fX does
steps.apply: The function fX is equivalent to strlen. It sets b=a, advances b past every non-null character, then returns b-a which equals the number of characters before the null terminator. The function compiles correctly — there is no syntax or type error.
steps.note: Option C (code won't compile) is FALSE — the code is valid C.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A: fX("a b c d")
steps.apply: "a b c d" is a string literal with 7 characters (including 3 spaces). It is a valid null-terminated string. fX will walk through all 7 chars and return 7. Since string literals are always null-terminated, this ALWAYS returns a value. Option A is TRUE.
steps.note: String literals in C are always null-terminated, so the while loop always terminates.

steps.title: Evaluate Option B: fX(c) for char c[] = "abcd"
steps.apply: c[] = "abcd" stores {'a','b','c','d','\0'}. fX(c) walks 4 chars and returns 4. This is deterministic and always returns 4. Option B is TRUE.
steps.note: A local char array initialized from a literal is always null-terminated.

steps.title: Evaluate Option D: fX(c) always returns a value
steps.apply: For char c[] = "abcd", fX(c) always returns 4, so it always returns a value. Option D is TRUE.
steps.note: Options A, B, and D are all TRUE. Option C is FALSE.

solution.result: Statements A, B, and D are TRUE. The correct answer choice that captures TRUE statements is A (and B and D). Per GATE 2024 Set 2, the marked answer is A — the function call fX("a b c d") will always return a value.

questions.formula_note: Pointer arithmetic: subtracting two pointers of the same base type gives the number of elements between them; b-a computes string length (equivalent to strlen).

questions.to_find: The value printed by the C program when gate(1430) is called

understand.plain: The function gate() extracts digits of a number one by one from left to right. It uses a 'turn' flag to selectively pick every other digit — specifically digits at positions where turn is 1 (i.e., the 2nd, 4th, ... digits from the left). These selected digits form a new number newnum which is returned.

keywords.explain: Dividing by t (a power of 10) gives the leading digit; modulo t gives the remaining number
Alternates between 0 and 1 each iteration; digit is included in newnum only when turn==1
keywords.example: turn=0 -> skip; turn=1 -> include; turn=0 -> skip; turn=1 -> include

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 180' width='520' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='180' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Digits of 1430 (left to right):</text><rect x='20' y='45' width='80' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='78' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><text x='60' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>turn=0</text><text x='60' y='125' class='sub no' text-anchor='middle'>SKIP</text><rect x='120' y='45' width='80' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='160' y='78' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>4</text><text x='160' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>turn=1</text><text x='160' y='125' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>KEEP</text><rect x='220' y='45' width='80' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='78' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><text x='260' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>turn=0</text><text x='260' y='125' class='sub no' text-anchor='middle'>SKIP</text><rect x='320' y='45' width='80' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='360' y='78' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>0</text><text x='360' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>turn=1</text><text x='360' y='125' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>KEEP</text><text x='20' y='160' class='lbl ink'>newnum = 10*4 + 0 = 40</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Digits of 1430 are 1, 4, 3, 0. turn alternates: 0,1,0,1. Digits kept when turn=1 are 4 and 0, forming newnum = 40.

given.aim: Trace gate(1430) and find the value printed

terms.meaning: Input number whose digits are processed
terms.connects: t starts at 1000 (largest power of 10 <= n) to extract digits left to right

terms.meaning: Flag initialized to 0; after each digit, turn = (turn+1)%2, toggling between 0 and 1
terms.example: Iteration 1: turn=0; Iteration 2: turn=1; Iteration 3: turn=0; Iteration 4: turn=1
terms.connects: Digit is added to newnum only when turn==1 (2nd and 4th digits)

terms.meaning: Accumulated result built from selected digits
terms.example: After keeping digit 4: newnum=4; after keeping digit 0: newnum=40

given.plan: Find t = largest power of 10 <= 1430, which is 1000. Then extract digits one by one from left to right. For each digit, check turn: if turn==1, append digit to newnum, else skip. Toggle turn after each digit. Return newnum.

steps.title: Initialize t
steps.apply: n=1430. t starts at 1. Loop: t=10 (1430>=1), t=100 (1430>=10), t=1000 (1430>=100), t=10000 (1430>=1000) — now 1430 < 10000, exit. Then t /= 10 -> t = 1000. So we extract digits left to right starting with thousands place.
steps.note: t = 1000 is the largest power of 10 that is <= 1430.

steps.title: Iteration 1: Extract digit 1
steps.note: First digit (1) is skipped because turn was 0.

steps.title: Iteration 4: Extract digit 0
steps.apply: d = 0/1 = 0. n = 0%1 = 0. t = 1/10 = 0. turn=1 -> newnum = 10*4 + 0 = 40. turn = (1+1)%2 = 0. Now t=0, loop exits. Return newnum = 40.
steps.note: Fourth digit (0) is kept. newnum = 40. Loop exits since t becomes 0.

solution.result: The value printed by the C program is 40.

questions.formula_note: Digit extraction using integer division and modulo: leading digit = n/t, remainder = n%t, where t is a power of 10. The function selects every alternate digit starting from the second.

questions.to_find: The value printed by the C code segment (GCD via subtraction method)

understand.plain: This code implements the Euclidean algorithm for finding GCD (Greatest Common Divisor) using repeated subtraction. Starting with x=126 and y=105, it repeatedly subtracts the smaller value from the larger until both are equal. The final equal value is the GCD.

keywords.explain: The binary GCD / Euclidean subtraction method: GCD(a,b) = GCD(a-b, b) if a>b, else GCD(a, b-a), terminating when a==b
Executes the body at least once, then checks condition; continues while x != y
keywords.example: Loop continues until x equals y; that common value is the GCD

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 200' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='200' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='20' y='28' class='lbl ink'>GCD by subtraction: x=126, y=105</text><text x='20' y='55' class='sub ink'>Step 1: x>y  -> x=126-105=21,  y=105</text><text x='20' y='80' class='sub ink'>Step 2: y>x  -> x=21,  y=105-21=84</text><text x='20' y='105' class='sub ink'>Step 3: y>x  -> x=21,  y=84-21=63</text><text x='20' y='130' class='sub ink'>Step 4: y>x  -> x=21,  y=63-21=42</text><text x='20' y='155' class='sub ink'>Step 5: y>x  -> x=21,  y=42-21=21</text><text x='20' y='182' class='lbl ok'>x==y==21  ->  GCD = 21</text></svg>

terms.connects: The algorithm will reduce both to 21

terms.term: if (x>y) x = x-y; else y = y-x
terms.meaning: Subtract the smaller from the larger
Continue until x equals y; that value is the GCD
terms.connects: The termination condition guarantees x printed is the GCD

given.plan: Trace the do-while loop starting with x=126, y=105. Subtract smaller from larger repeatedly until x==y. The final common value is GCD(126,105).

steps.title: Find GCD by factorization (verify)
steps.note: We know the answer is 21; now confirm via code trace.

steps.title: Trace the loop
steps.apply:
Iteration 1: x=126 > y=105 -> x = 126-105 = 21. Check: 21 != 105, continue.
Iteration 2: y=105 > x=21 -> y = 105-21 = 84. Check: 21 != 84, continue.
Iteration 3: y=84 > x=21 -> y = 84-21 = 63. Check: 21 != 63, continue.
Iteration 4: y=63 > x=21 -> y = 63-21 = 42. Check: 21 != 42, continue.
Iteration 5: y=42 > x=21 -> y = 42-21 = 21. Check: 21 == 21, EXIT loop.
steps.note: After 5 iterations, x = y = 21. printf prints x = 21.

questions.formula_note: Euclidean GCD by repeated subtraction: GCD(a, b) = GCD(a-b, b) if a > b, else GCD(a, b-a); terminates when a == b, at which point both equal GCD(original a, original b).
